verbatim_text: Now you're not going to experiment with your different ratios of ingredients since you don't know how to bake a cake at scale. And you're not going to create 10 different ginormous messes of wedding cakes where only maybe one or two of them actually works out. This is a bad way to experiment because it's very expensive. So instead, since you don't know how to bake a cake and you're trying out different recipes, you're going to scale things down and you're going to bake a bunch of muffins, say. And you're going to figure out what the right recipe is. And this is a much more efficient way of figuring out what to do in this situation because you're not doing it at scale. You don't waste as many resources. And so after which you're going to scale up, use that recipe and make your big wedding cake. Great. And so here's a real scaling loss plot. So on the x-axis here, we have the amount of compute you put in to producing your model. In this case, this is the amount of compute used for pre-training your foundation model. And on the y-axis, you have the ratio of two different decisions that you needed to put into this recipe. You need the number of tokens that you're going to use to train this thing and the number of parameters that are in the model, how big it is.
